title: Debt traps and scams
description: Spot the traps that eat paychecks — minimum payments, payday loans, and scammers in costume.
summaryPoints: Pay credit cards in full — minimum payments can turn a $500 purchase into $700+
Payday loans are a trap: borrow $200, owe $250 just two weeks later
Scams share two tells: too good to be true, and in a big hurry
Never pay an upfront fee or share a PIN to 'claim' or 'activate' anything
Verify through a door YOU open: the official app, the listed number

content.title: Two traps, one rule
content.body:
Money has two kinds of predators: **debt traps**, which you walk into, and **scams**, which are thrown at you.

Both run on the same fuel — speed. Sign now, claim now, buy now, act before you think. So both have the same antidote: slow down and run the numbers.

By the end of this lesson you'll be able to smell pressure-money from across the room.

content.title: The minimum payment trap
content.body:
A credit card is a loan in your pocket. Pay the full balance every month and it costs you nothing. Carry a balance, and interest — around 20–25% APR — starts feeding.

The sneakiest setting is the **minimum payment**. It feels helpful ('only $15 this month!') but it's designed to stretch your debt across years: a $500 shopping spree paid in minimums can quietly end up costing over $700.
content.imageAlt: Spiral of minimum payments pulling money downward

content.prompt: If you pay your credit card balance in full every month, the borrowed money costs you nothing in interest.
content.explanation: Full payment by the due date means no interest at all. The trap only springs when a balance rolls over — that's when 20–25% APR kicks in.

content.prompt: A $500 shopping spree on a credit card, paying only minimums. Slide to roughly what it could cost you in total.
content.explanation: Minimums stretch a $500 purchase across years while interest piles up — past $700 in total. The slowest, most expensive way to pay.

content.title: Payday loans and borrowing rules
content.body:
**Payday loans** are the sharpest trap of all: borrow $200 today, owe $250 in two weeks. Can't pay? Borrow again — and now the cycle owns you.

**Student and car loans** can be smart — they buy things that grow your future. But only with a repayment plan you've actually run the numbers on.

The borrowing rules:
- Only borrow what you can realistically repay
- Know the interest rate and schedule BEFORE signing
- Never borrow on impulse for a want

content.context: You want an $800 phone. You've saved $200. A friend says a payday loan can get you the rest today.
options: Take the payday loan — you'll figure out repayment
Put the missing $600 on a credit card and pay minimums
Keep saving until you can buy it outright
content.explanation: Payday fees are brutal ($200 borrowed → $250 owed in two weeks) and minimums quietly inflate the price. Patience is the only option that charges no interest.

content.prompt: Payday loan math: borrow $200 today, owe $___ just two weeks later.
content.explanation: A $50 fee on $200 in 14 days is far steeper than even credit card rates — and many borrowers re-borrow to cover it. That's the cycle.

content.title: Scams are costumes
content.body:
Every scam plays the same trick — grab your money or your info — in a different outfit:
- **Phishing** — fake 'bank' messages asking you to log in or 'verify'
- **Fake jobs** — amazing pay, no interview, then an upfront 'training fee'
- **Prize scams** — you 'won' something, but must pay or share details to claim it
- **Marketplace scams** — pay first, product never existed

The tell under every costume: too good to be true, and in a big hurry.
content.imageAlt: Phishing hook reaching for a phone

content.prompt: Match each scam to its tell-tale sign.

pairs.right: Fake bank message wants your login

content.prompt: A dream job lands in your inbox. Tap all 3 red flags.

segments.text: Hi! We reviewed your profile and you're a perfect fit for our team.
No interview needed — you start tomorrow at $45/hr for simple data entry.
segments.flag: Real jobs don't skip interviews or overpay for easy work. That's the bait.

segments.text: To activate your work account, send a one-time $60 training fee
segments.flag: Legit employers pay YOU. Any upfront fee means the 'job' is the scam.

segments.text: via gift cards within the next 2 hours.
segments.flag: Gift cards plus a countdown: untraceable money collected under panic pressure.

segments.text: Welcome aboard — we're excited to work with you!

content.explanation: Jobs that chase you, skip interviews, and charge entry fees aren't jobs. Look the company up and call its listed number — or just delete.

content.title: Your anti-trap toolkit
content.body:
Five habits that keep both predators hungry:
- **Budget first** — planned money doesn't borrow on impulse
- **Card rule** — only charge what you can pay in full this month
- **Even a tiny emergency fund** — $50–$100 stops a surprise from becoming a loan
- **Check statements weekly** — fraud caught early is fraud reversed
- **Strong, unique passwords** on every money account

content.prompt: Scam red flag or perfectly normal?

items.text: Pay a fee to unlock your prize
A receipt emailed after you buy something
An interview before a job offer
Reply in 10 minutes or lose the deal
A stranger 'overpays' you and asks for a refund
Your bank app asks for your password when YOU open it

content.context: You just got your first credit card. You want $100 of new clothes and need $50 of groceries before payday next week.
options: Charge everything, pay the minimum next month
Groceries on the card — paid in full on payday — and keep saving cash for the clothes
Clothes on the card now; groceries can wait a week
Charge both and worry about it later
content.explanation: Needs first, and only charge what you can clear in full. That keeps the card a convenience instead of a debt machine.

content.instructions: One word makes this dangerously wrong. Tap it.
content.sentence: Minimum payments are the fastest way to escape credit card debt.
content.explanation: Minimums mostly feed interest — they're designed to keep you in debt longer, not get you out.

content.title: Before you tap reply
content.body:
One habit defeats nearly every scam: **verify through a door YOU open**.

Message from 'your bank'? Open the real app yourself. Surprise job offer? Look the company up and call its listed number. 'You won'? Ask yourself: did I even enter?

Nobody legitimate pressures you to skip that check — the more urgent the message, the more suspicious you should be.

Final exam: a 'prize' text is about to land. Find every red flag.

content.prompt: This text says you won $500. Tap all 3 red flags.

segments.text: Congratulations! Your number was chosen in our $500 customer giveaway.
You must claim within 30 minutes or the prize will be forfeited.
segments.flag: Manufactured urgency — real organizations don't give 30-minute deadlines.

segments.text: To receive the funds, reply with your bank account number and card PIN.
segments.flag: No real prize ever needs your account number or PIN. That's the theft itself.
A sketchy, unfamiliar domain — links like this exist to steal logins or install junk.

segments.text: Thank you for being a loyal customer.

content.explanation: You can't win a contest you never entered. 'Free money' that needs your bank info first isn't free — it's bait. Delete, block, move on.
